title: YouTube Channel Performance Analysis
text:
This document describes the methodology behind three core YouTube performance analyses applied to a dataset containing video-level metrics (views, likes, comments, publish timestamps) across two channels.
Dataset Fields:




Field
Description




video_id
Unique YouTube video identifier


channel
Channel identifier (1 or 2)


title
Video title


published_at
UTC timestamp of publication


views
Total view count


likes
Total like count


comments
Total comment count


last_updated
Timestamp of data collection






Videos with zero views cause division-by-zero errors (NaN, Inf) when computing rates. Always clean before proceeding:
Why NA_real_ instead of filtering? Using NA_real_ preserves all rows in the dataset while safely excluding zero-view videos from rate calculations. Most R functions like mean() and scale() accept na.rm = TRUE to handle these gracefully.






Engagement analysis quantifies how actively viewers interact with content relative to how many people watch it. A video can have high views but low engagement (passive audience) or low views but high engagement (niche but dedicated audience). Both patterns carry strategic insight.












Metric
Formula
Interpretation




Like Rate
likes / views
Proportion of viewers who liked


Comment Rate
comments / views
Proportion of viewers who commented


Engagement Rate
(likes + comments) / views
Overall interaction rate





Why filter views >= 100? A video with 1 like and 5 views has a 20% engagement rate — statistically meaningless. Setting a minimum view threshold ensures comparisons are fair and representative.





Engagement rate > 5% — strong audience connection
Engagement rate 2–5% — typical for religious/liturgical content
Engagement rate < 1% — passive viewership, content may not be resonating





Look for consistent peaks in the heatmap — these are your best publishing windows
Be cautious of hours with very few videos (small n) — averages can be misleading with few data points
Sunday peaks are expected for liturgical content; compare weekday patterns for non-Sunday services







This analysis identifies videos that deviate significantly from the channel’s typical performance pattern. It surfaces four types of anomalies:








Flag
Meaning




High Views, Low Engagement
Many viewers but little interaction — audience may be passive or content misaligned


Low Views, High Engagement
Small but highly engaged audience — potential niche content worth promoting


Top Performer
High on both dimensions — replicate this content


Underperformer
Low on both dimensions — review for quality or distribution issues


Normal
Within expected range







A z-score measures how far a value is from the channel’s mean, in units of standard deviation:
z = (value - mean) / standard_deviation

z > 1 means the video is above average (top ~16%)
z < -1 means the video is below average (bottom ~16%)
Calculated per channel so each channel is benchmarked against itself




If z_engage produces NaN values, the cause is almost always zero-view videos producing Inf or NaN in engage_rate before scaling. Diagnose and fix:




Red points (High Views, Low Engagement): Investigate — was the video shared externally (driving passive viewers)? Is the content format different from usual?
Blue points (Low Views, High Engagement): Consider promoting these — the core audience loves them
Green points (Top Performers): Study these carefully and replicate their format, length, and timing
Gray points (Underperformers): Review for technical issues (poor audio/video quality, misleading titles)





Analysis methodology based on 2,344 videos published between 2018–2025 across 2 Ethiopian Orthodox church channels.
